questions.question: Describe the step-by-step process of inserting a new node into a sorted singly linked list. Include a discussion of the edge cases that must be handled.
keyPoints: Traverse the list to find the correct position where the new node's data is greater than the previous node and less than the next node.
Handle the edge case where the list is empty by making the new node the head.
Handle the edge case where the new node needs to be inserted at the beginning of the list (becomes the new head).

questions.question: Explain the algorithm for finding the minimum value in a Binary Search Tree (BST). Why is this operation efficient, and what is its time complexity?
keyPoints: The minimum value is found by traversing the left subtree recursively until a node with no left child is found.
This operation is efficient because it only traverses the height of the tree along the leftmost branch.
The time complexity is O(h), where h is the height of the tree. In a balanced BST, this is O(log n).

questions.question: Compare and contrast the iterative and recursive methods for counting all nodes in a linked list. Discuss the potential advantages and disadvantages of each approach in terms of time complexity, space complexity, and practical implementation.
keyPoints: Both methods have an O(n) time complexity as they must visit each node once.
The iterative method uses O(1) auxiliary space, while the recursive method uses O(n) space on the call stack due to recursive function calls, which risks a stack overflow for very long lists.
A practical disadvantage of recursion is its inherent overhead and lack of tail-call optimization in many languages, making iteration generally more efficient and preferred for this simple operation.
